messages.say:
what’s the correct mental model between a server, a sidecar, spinning up multiple processes from one deployed machine, services, and multiple servers


bun/node

Start with the clean split: you are mixing three different axes.


One axis is process topology: how many operating system processes are running.
Another is network topology: how many ports/endpoints/servers are accepting requests.
Another is logical architecture: how many “services” exist as separate responsibilities.


Those are related, but not the same thing. Most confusion comes from collapsing them.


The most useful mental model in Bun or Node is this:


A server is usually a process that listens for requests on a port or socket. In Bun/Node terms, that is your Bun.serve(...), http.createServer(...), Fastify app, Elysia app, and so on. If something is actually binding to :3000, that thing is a server.


A process is just a running operating system program. One deployed machine can run one process or twenty. A process may be a server, or it may be a worker, scheduler, cron loop, queue consumer, file watcher, etc. Not every process is a server.


A service is mostly a logical unit of capability. “Auth service,” “billing service,” “email service,” “search service.” Sometimes that maps to one process. Sometimes several services live inside one process. Sometimes one service spans multiple processes. “Service” is an architecture word, not a precise runtime word.


A sidecar is a companion process deployed alongside a main process, usually on the same machine, container group, or pod, and tightly coupled to it operationally. It is not just “another process.” It exists to support the main app: proxying, telemetry, secrets injection, log shipping, service mesh, local queue bridge, file sync, etc.


A machine is the host. One VM, one container host, one bare-metal box, one Railway instance depending on platform semantics. Multiple processes can run on one machine. Multiple servers can run on one machine, each on different ports.


Now the clean mapping.


If you have one Bun app listening on port 3000, that is:


1 machine
1 process
1 server
maybe 1 service, maybe many logical services inside it


If you have an API on port 3000 and a background worker in another Bun process on the same machine, that is:


1 machine
2 processes
1 server + 1 worker
maybe still 1 product/system
possibly 2 logical services, depending on boundaries


If you have API on 3000 and admin panel backend on 3001, both on same machine:


1 machine
2 processes
2 servers
possibly 2 services


If you have API on 3000 and an OpenTelemetry Collector or Envoy proxy next to it:


1 machine
2 processes
1 main app server + 1 sidecar


That sidecar may itself also be a server internally, but the reason it exists is what matters. “Sidecar” describes the deployment relationship, not whether it listens on a port.


Here is the wrong mental model that breaks people:


“Every service is a server and every server is a different machine.”


False.


A single Node/Bun monolith can contain many services logically. One machine can host many processes. One process can expose multiple routes and act as several logical services. A sidecar can be a server, but not every adjacent server is a sidecar.


The better mental model is to think in layers.


At the bottom is the host:


Machine / container / VM
  -> processes
      -> some processes open ports (servers)
      -> some processes do background work


On top of that is architecture:


Product/system
  -> logical services/capabilities
      -> implemented by one or more processes


That is the separation you want.


In Bun/Node specifically, these are the common runtime shapes.


The simplest shape is the single-process app. One Bun process. One server. Routes handle API, maybe even simple scheduled jobs. Good for small systems. Breaks when background work becomes long-running, failure-prone, or resource-heavy.


Next is API + worker. One process serves requests, another consumes jobs. This is usually the first real split that matters. Not because “microservices,” but because request/response latency and background execution have different lifecycles. If image processing or emails or imports can crash or stall, keep them out of the web server process.


Then API + worker + sidecar(s). Example: Bun API, Bun worker, local OpenTelemetry Collector sidecar, maybe Redis or a reverse proxy on the same machine. This is operational composition, not necessarily distributed architecture.


Then multiple independent servers/services. Separate auth server, API server, workflow server, websocket server. They may run on one machine in dev, and different machines in prod. The mental model should survive that transition.


A sidecar deserves extra precision because people misuse it.


A real sidecar usually has these properties:
it is deployed next to one primary app instance, it has a narrow support role, it often shares lifecycle with the app, and the app can often talk to it over localhost or shared files/network namespace.


Examples:
an Nginx reverse proxy in front of your Node app,
an OpenTelemetry Collector receiving traces from your app,
an Envoy proxy,
a local secrets agent,
a file-sync/log-forwarding helper.


A database is usually not called a sidecar. Redis usually is not a sidecar unless you are very specifically running an instance solely to support that app instance locally. More often those are just backing services.


That distinction matters:


A backing service is a dependency your app talks to.
A sidecar is a companion process deployed with your app.


Examples:


Node API -> Postgres


Postgres is a backing service, not a sidecar.


Node API -> localhost:4317 OpenTelemetry Collector


The collector is a sidecar.


Now the subtle part: multiple processes from one deployed machine.


This is completely normal. The machine is just capacity. Processes are isolated runtimes. You can run:


bun api.ts
bun worker.ts
bun scheduler.ts
bun websocket.ts


all on one box.


That does not automatically mean you have “microservices.” You may just have one system with several runtime roles. Whether they are separate services depends on whether they have independent contracts, ownership boundaries, deployment cadence, scaling needs, and failure domains.


That gives you the practical test:


If two pieces must always deploy together, share the same codebase, same database, same ownership, same release timing, and mostly just have different runtime roles, they are probably not separate services. They are separate processes in one service or one system.


If two pieces can be versioned, scaled, deployed, observed, and reasoned about independently, they are closer to separate services.


A useful matrix:


Term       | What it describes
-----------|------------------------------
Machine    | Host/runtime environment
Process    | OS execution unit
Server     | Process that listens for requests
Service    | Logical capability/boundary
Sidecar    | Companion support process deployed next to a main app


Now the Bun/Node-specific traps.


First trap: “Node is single-threaded, so one machine means one thing.”
Wrong. One Node/Bun process has one main event loop, but you can run multiple processes easily. cluster, process managers, containers, separate deploy commands, and platform process formation all exist.


Second trap: “If I spawn another process from my app, that means sidecar.”
Not really. That usually just means child process or worker process. A sidecar is an operational/deployment pattern, not a JavaScript API choice.


Third trap: “If two servers run on different ports, they are different services.”
Not necessarily. They may just be split entrypoints. Port count is not architecture.


Fourth trap: “A service package in code should become a separate deployed server.”
No. Code packaging and deployment packaging do not need 1:1 alignment. You can keep strong service boundaries in code and still deploy as one server/process until scale or isolation demands a split.


Here is the sanity check I would use.


Ask five questions:


1. Does it listen on its own port/socket?
   -> then it is a server.

2. Is it a separate OS runtime?
   -> then it is a separate process.

3. Is it mainly there to support another process locally?
   -> then it is probably a sidecar.

4. Is it a distinct business/system capability?
   -> then it may be a service.

5. Can it be deployed and scaled independently without lying to yourself?
   -> then it is more truly its own service/server unit.


Concrete examples.


Example 1: Bun API plus queue consumer.


machine
  - process A: Bun API server on :3000
  - process B: Bun worker consumes jobs from Redis


Correct model: one machine, two processes, one server plus one worker, maybe one product with two runtime roles. Not necessarily two services.


Example 2: Bun API plus OpenTelemetry Collector.


machine
  - process A: Bun API server on :3000
  - process B: otel collector on :4317


Correct model: API server plus sidecar.


Example 3: Auth server and application server on same box.


machine
  - process A: auth server on :3001
  - process B: app server on :3000


Correct model: two servers, two processes, maybe two services, same machine.


Example 4: One big Elysia app with /auth, /billing, /search.


Correct model: one server, one process, multiple logical services/modules inside one deployable app.


That is usually the right default for small and mid-sized Bun/Node systems: keep logical boundaries strong and runtime/deployment boundaries weak until needed.


So the mental model I would actually carry forward is this:


Build in code as if services are logical boundaries.
Deploy initially as few processes as possible.
Split into separate processes when runtime behavior differs.
Split into separate servers when network/API isolation is needed.
Call something a sidecar only when it is an operational companion to a main app instance.


The practical default stack for most Bun/Node projects is:


Start:
  1 process, 1 server

Then:
  1 server process + 1 worker process

Then maybe:
  + observability/proxy sidecar

Only later:
  multiple independently deployed servers/services


That path preserves simplicity without lying about architecture.


The baseline sanity check is this: if you say “service” and cannot answer whether you mean code boundary, process boundary, or deployment boundary, your model is too fuzzy and will cause bad architecture decisions.


The fix is to always name the layer explicitly:
“service module,” “worker process,” “API server,” “sidecar collector,” “shared host.”


That alone removes most of the confusion.


If you want, I’ll map this directly onto your RAWR/plugin/service/host model and show which pieces should stay logical-only versus become separate processes.
Why is a bun worker not a sidecar?

Because “worker” and “sidecar” describe different things.


A worker describes a runtime role: it does background work instead of serving normal request/response traffic.


A sidecar describes a deployment relationship: it is a companion process attached to a main app instance to support it operationally.


Those can overlap, but they are not the same category.


A Bun worker is usually just another application process you wrote. It runs your business logic: send emails, process jobs, execute workflows, consume queue events, run imports, retry failed tasks. That makes it a worker.


A sidecar usually exists to support the main app rather than to embody core product/business logic. Think OpenTelemetry Collector, Envoy proxy, Nginx reverse proxy, secrets agent, log shipper. Those are infrastructure-adjacent helpers.


So the real distinction is not “does it run next to another process?” It is “what is its job in the system?”


Here is the clean test.


Your Bun worker is not a sidecar when:


it contains core application logic

it could reasonably be scaled independently based on workload

it may consume from shared queues across many app instances

it is part of the product/system itself, not just a helper for one local app instance


Example:


api server -> enqueues "generate report"
worker -> generates report, writes DB row, emails user


That worker is part of the application. It is not just helping the API server exist. It is doing real product work.


A process is more like a sidecar when:


it is tightly paired to one app instance

it mainly exists to augment that instance

it is usually not where your business logic lives

if you removed the main app, the sidecar would no longer have a reason to exist in that form


Example:


bun api -> sends traces to localhost collector
otel collector -> batches/exports traces


The collector is a sidecar.


The confusion happens because both may run on the same machine, and both may be “adjacent.” But adjacency is not enough.


This is the deeper mental model:


Worker   = what kind of work the process does
Sidecar  = why that process is colocated with another one


So a Bun worker can be deployed beside an API and still not be a sidecar. It is just a sibling process with a different role.


There is one edge case: a worker can be deployed as a sidecar pattern if it is strictly local to a single app instance and exists only to support that instance. For example, a local file-processing helper that only serves one API instance through local IPC (inter-process communication). Then you could describe it as both:


functionally: a worker

topologically: a sidecar


But that is not the usual meaning when people say “background worker” in Node/Bun systems.


Sanity check:


If the process disappeared, would the system lose a core product capability?
- yes -> probably an app worker/service process
- no, mostly observability/networking/support -> probably a sidecar


That is the practical distinction.


In your architecture conversations, calling a Bun worker a sidecar too early will break your thinking because it makes core application runtimes sound like optional support helpers. They are not. A worker is usually part of the application topology, not a sidecar to it.

The cleanest categorization is: do not treat these as equivalent “plugin kinds.” They live on different axes.


In this repo, the target model is already explicit: shared capability logic lives in packages/*, runtime adapters live in plugins/*, final assembly lives in rawr.hq.ts, and hosts in apps/* mount those surfaces rather than inventing their own capability wiring


PLUGINS


[


SYSTEM


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/docs/SYSTEM.md). The current manifest already shows the shape: one composed oRPC router, one workflow trigger router, and one host-owned Inngest bundle per process[


rawr.hq


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/rawr.hq.ts).


So the right mental model is not “plugin type = deployment type.” It is:


packages/* = capability/kernel/domain

plugins/* = surface adapters

apps/* = actual hosts/processes

rawr.hq.ts = composition authority


From that, here is how I would categorize each.


API plugins


These are boundary-surface adapters for the shared server host. They are not workers, not sidecars, and not standalone services by default. They adapt package capabilities into caller-facing oRPC/OpenAPI routes. That is already the documented target: plugins/api/* own boundary contracts/routers, and the server mounts them into /rpc and /api/orpc/* via manifest composition


PLUGINS


[


ARCHITECTURE


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/docs/projects/orpc-ingest-workflows-spec/ARCHITECTURE.md)[


rawr


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/apps/server/src/rawr.ts).


So category:


logical category: boundary adapter

runtime category: server-mounted plugin

deployment category: usually in the main server process

process model: not a sidecar, not a worker


Workflow plugins


These are dual-surface plugins. This is the one people blur.


A workflow plugin has:


a caller-facing trigger surface mounted under /api/workflows/<capability>/*

a durable execution surface implemented as Inngest functions behind /api/inngest


That split is explicitly locked in the spec: API boundary and durable execution remain split; workflow triggers are caller-facing; /api/inngest is runtime ingress only; one runtime-owned Inngest bundle exists per process


ARCHITECTURE


. The manifest reflects that by exporting both workflows.triggerRouter and inngest.{ client, functions, handler }[


rawr.hq


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/rawr.hq.ts), and the server host mounts them in that order[


rawr


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/apps/server/src/rawr.ts).


So category:


logical category: durable-workflow adapter

runtime category: split between boundary trigger surface and workflow runtime surface

deployment category: usually shared with the server host at first, but conceptually the durable half is closer to a worker runtime

process model: not a sidecar; it is part server adapter, part durable worker topology


This is the key distinction: a workflow plugin is not “just another API plugin.” It straddles triggering and execution.


CLI plugins


These are local command-surface adapters. They are not server plugins at all. They adapt package capabilities into command invocations through the rawr entrypoint. The docs already call out plugins/cli/* as a plugin root and rawr.hq.ts as the place where CLI command registries are composed for host mounting


SYSTEM


.


So category:


logical category: operator/developer command adapter

runtime category: local process entry surface

deployment category: runs in the CLI host process when invoked

process model: ephemeral foreground process, not a server, not a sidecar, not a worker by default


Cron plugins


I would not make cron a peer semantic category beside API, workflows, and CLI.


Cron is not really a surface. It is a trigger mode.


If the cron job exists to kick off durable execution, it belongs under workflow plugins. The schedule is just another ingress into the workflow runtime. Inngest already naturally fits this shape. So “cron workflow” is a workflow plugin with a schedule trigger, not a new top-level plugin kind.


Only make a distinct cron plugin kind if you are saying, very specifically, “this thing is host-scheduled and not part of the durable workflow system.” That is a weaker, less coherent model.


So my recommendation:


do not create plugins/cron/* as a primary kind

treat cron as either:
a workflow trigger mode, or

a host scheduler registration attached to a worker/background runtime


Background plugins


This is too vague to be a good primary kind.


“Background” describes execution posture, not interface shape. It tells you that something should not block a request, but it does not tell you whether the thing is:


scheduled,

queue-driven,

event-driven,

durable,

ephemeral,

in-process,

isolated in another process.


So I would avoid background as a canonical plugin root. It is a smell category, like naming something utils.


If you keep it at all, it should be a second-order classification inside a more concrete kind:


workflow plugin with background execution

worker plugin with background consumption

API plugin that enqueues background work


Worker plugins


This one can be legitimate, but only if you define it sharply.


A worker plugin should mean: a plugin whose primary role is runtime execution/consumption, not boundary exposure. That could mean queue consumers, stream consumers, daemon loops, or continuously running processors. Unlike workflow plugins, which are specifically durable-execution adapters around an Inngest-style model, worker plugins would cover non-Inngest long-running runtime roles.


So category:


logical category: execution/runtime adapter

runtime category: non-boundary process role

deployment category: usually separate worker process, though could share host initially

process model: sibling application process, not sidecar by default


This is where your earlier sidecar question matters. A worker plugin is usually not a sidecar because it carries core application behavior, can scale independently, and is part of the product/system runtime rather than merely supporting another app instance.


The real categorization stack I would use in RAWR is this:


Primary plugin kinds by surface


api = caller-facing boundary adapter

workflows = durable trigger + execution adapter

cli = command adapter

web = frontend/web runtime adapter

agents = agent capability adapter

mcp = MCP action adapter


That matches the current docs exactly


PLUGINS


[


SYSTEM


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/docs/SYSTEM.md).


Then add a separate runtime-role axis:


Runtime roles


server-mounted

worker-executed

scheduled

ephemeral-cli

browser/web

agent-runtime


That is the missing clarity.


So if you expand the system, I would categorize your proposed additions like this:


API plugin: surface kind

Workflow plugin: surface kind with durable runtime

CLI plugin: surface kind

Cron: trigger mode, not primary kind

Background: execution posture, not primary kind

Worker: runtime role or possibly a distinct kind only for non-workflow execution plugins


That means the good model is not:


api | workflows | cli | cron | background | worker


That is mixing surface kinds with runtime roles and trigger styles.


The better model is:


Surface kinds:
api | workflows | cli | web | agents | mcp

Runtime roles / execution modes:
server-mounted | worker | scheduled | background | interactive


Then the combinations become obvious:


API plugin → server-mounted

Workflow plugin → server-mounted trigger surface + worker/durable execution runtime

CLI plugin → interactive/ephemeral

Scheduled workflow → workflow plugin + scheduled trigger

Queue consumer → worker plugin or workflow plugin, depending on whether it is Inngest-native durable execution

Background email sender → usually workflow plugin or worker runtime, not “background plugin”


Sanity check against the repo’s current host model:


The server host today mounts /api/inngest, /api/workflows/*, and /rpc + /api/orpc/* in explicit order


rawr


. The manifest is already the single authority that composes oRPC routes and Inngest runtime seams[


rawr.hq


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/rawr.hq.ts). That means your architecture already wants surface-first classification with host-owned runtime composition. Lean into that.


My blunt recommendation:


Do not add background as a top-level plugin type.
Do not add cron as a top-level plugin type.
Only add worker as a top-level plugin type if you need a real non-Inngest execution surface.


Otherwise:


cron belongs to workflows

background belongs to runtime semantics

worker is either workflows or a separate execution host class


The strongest version of the model is:


plugins/* should classify how the system is extended at an interface/runtime seam, not every incidental way code happens to run.


If you want, I can turn this into a concrete proposed directory + manifest contract for plugins/workers/* versus folding scheduled/background execution into plugins/workflows/*.

Lets say we expect to grow a little bit. Right now everything runs on one host. However, I can imagine that the host for the server vs. the async execution runs sent by inngest vs. background worker plugins may want their own processes or machines in the future. I don’t know.


What would the minimal yet performant architecture for this look like? Or is it really all fine running on “one host”? Should we keep one “host” mental model such that 1 plugin system : 1 host and the plugin types don’t matter? Or does that break?

The right answer is: keep one HQ/plugin system, but do not collapse that into “there is one host forever, so plugin types don’t matter.”


That breaks as soon as runtime behavior diverges.


The clean model is:


1 HQ/plugin system
N host roles


Not:


1 plugin system = 1 physical host/process forever


Your repo is already pointing in that direction. rawr.hq.ts is the single composition authority for surfaces and runtime seams, while host apps mount those exports instead of re-authoring capability wiring


rawr.hq


[


SYSTEM


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/docs/SYSTEM.md). The workflow architecture also explicitly separates boundary APIs from durable execution and says there is one runtime-owned Inngest bundle per process, not one globally forever[


ARCHITECTURE


](https://github.com/rawr-ai/rawr-hq-template/blob/codex/agent-codex-gitnexus-install/docs/projects/orpc-ingest-workflows-spec/ARCHITECTURE.md). That “per process” wording matters.


So the mental model that scales is:


one logical HQ

one plugin registry/composition authority

potentially several runtime hosts/process roles


Those roles can all run on one machine today and separate later without changing plugin semantics.


The minimal yet performant architecture is this:


Stage 1: one machine, two or three processes max


Keep everything on one box if the load is modest. That is fine. What matters is separating runtime roles, not necessarily physical machines.


The default shape I would use is:


Machine A
  - process 1: server host
      mounts:
      - /rpc
      - /api/orpc/*
      - /api/workflows/<capability>/*
      - /api/inngest (if needed initially)

  - process 2: async runtime host
      runs:
      - Inngest durable execution
      - background worker plugins
      - scheduled jobs / cron triggers

  - optional process 3: web host / asset host
      only if needed separately


This is minimal, because it is still one machine.
This is performant, because you isolate request/response latency from async execution.
This is scalable, because you can move process 2 to another machine later with minimal conceptual churn.


That is the architecture I would recommend.


Why not keep literally everything in one process?


Because the first real split in systems like this is not “microservices.” It is foreground vs background.


Your server host has different requirements:


low latency

stable memory

predictable tail latency

request-scoped auth/context

easy horizontal scaling later


Your async execution host has different requirements:


retries

long-running work

burst tolerance

looser latency requirements

separate concurrency controls

safe failure isolation


If you keep them in one process too long, this will break because:


a burst of async work will steal CPU and memory from user-facing requests

one bad workflow/plugin can destabilize the main server

concurrency tuning becomes impossible without compromising one side

observability gets muddier because foreground and background work share one pool and one failure domain


So “one host” is fine as a machine model for now.
It is not fine as a single runtime role model once you expect growth.


That means the best mental model is:


one system, one manifest, multiple host roles


I would define the host roles like this.


1. Server host


This is the caller-facing boundary host.


It mounts:


API plugins

workflow trigger routers

maybe web plugins if server-served

maybe CLI is separate, not here


Its job is:


accept requests

authenticate/authorize

validate contracts

enqueue or trigger async work

return responses quickly


In your current architecture, this corresponds to the mounted route families in apps/server/src/rawr.ts: /api/inngest, /api/workflows/*, and /rpc + /api/orpc/* in explicit order


rawr


. But for future shape, I would treat /api/inngest as the thing most likely to move off this host first in responsibility, even if the HTTP ingress point remains nearby.


2. Async execution host


This is the execution/runtime host.


It runs:


Inngest durable functions

worker plugins

scheduled triggers

queue consumers

long-running/background logic


Its job is not to be the public server.
Its job is to execute work reliably.


This host is where I would put:


workflow durable execution

worker plugins

cron/scheduled plugins


That gives you one clean runtime class for “non-request work.”


3. CLI host


This is already separate by nature. CLI plugins are not part of the long-running server topology. They are invoked processes. Treat them as their own host class conceptually, even if they run on the same machine during development.


Now the plugin categorization becomes much cleaner.


Do not think “plugin types don’t matter.”
They do matter, but not because each one deserves its own machine.


They matter because each plugin type attaches to one or more host roles.


A better mapping looks like this:


API plugins
  -> server host

Workflow plugins
  -> server host (trigger surface)
  -> async execution host (durable execution)

Worker plugins
  -> async execution host

Cron/scheduled behavior
  -> async execution host
  -> usually as a trigger mode, not a whole new top-level worldview

CLI plugins
  -> CLI host

Web plugins
  -> web host and/or server host depending on delivery model


That is the model that survives growth.


Now the practical question: is one machine still okay?


Yes, absolutely, if you split by process role.


A very good near-term setup is:


Railway service A: server
Railway service B: async
Optional Railway service C: web
Shared Postgres / Redis / backing services


Or if you truly want one deployed machine:


one VM/container host
  supervises:
    bun run server
    bun run async


That is enough for a long time.


The thing I would not do is make plugin type the unit of deployment. That becomes nonsense fast.


You do not want:


one process for each API plugin

one process for each workflow plugin

one process for each worker plugin


That is fake modularity and real ops pain.


The deployment unit should be host role, not plugin kind.


So the actual answer to your “1 plugin system : 1 host” question is:


Keep 1 plugin system : 1 composition authority.
Do not keep 1 plugin system : 1 runtime host forever.


That breaks once execution modes differ.


The best durable mental model is:


One HQ/plugin system
  -> one manifest/composition authority
  -> many plugin surfaces
  -> mounted into a small number of host roles
  -> each host role can run as one process today and separate later


That gives you minimal complexity now and a clean migration path later.


Here is the staged path I would recommend.


Stage A: now


one machine

one server process

one async process

same repo, same plugin system, same manifest authority


Stage B: moderate growth


separate deploy units for server and async

same plugin contracts

same rawr.hq composition semantics

host-specific mounting/build steps


Stage C: only if needed


isolate especially hot or risky worker classes

maybe dedicated workflow runner or heavy compute worker

maybe separate web serving

still do not split by plugin kind blindly


Here is the sanity check.


If a runtime role has different needs in:


latency,

concurrency,

failure tolerance,

scaling,

uptime,

or resource profile,


then it should probably be its own host process.


By that test:


server and async should be separate soon

CLI is already separate by nature

web may or may not be separate

individual plugin kinds usually should not each get their own process


My blunt recommendation:


Do not keep the mental model that plugin types do not matter.
Do not keep the mental model that one HQ equals one forever-host.


Keep:


one HQ

one manifest authority

two primary long-running host roles: server and async

optional web and CLI host roles as needed


That is the minimal architecture that is still honest.


The baseline version in concrete terms:


HQ / plugin system
  rawr.hq.ts = composition authority

Host roles
  server-host:
    mounts api + workflow triggers

  async-host:
    runs inngest durable execution + worker plugins + schedules
